1.title: Segmentation
1.desc: Divide an object into independent parts, or make it sectional, or increase the degree of fragmentation.
1.example: Modular furniture, multi-core cable, venetian blinds.

2.title: Taking Out
2.desc: Separate an interfering part or property from an object, or single out the only necessary part.
2.example: Locate a noisy compressor outside the building; use a remote control.

3.title: Local Quality
3.desc: Change an object's structure from uniform to non-uniform; make different parts perform different functions.
3.example: A pencil with an eraser; a hammer with a nail puller; non-stick coating only on the inside of a pan.

4.title: Asymmetry
4.desc: Change the shape of an object from symmetrical to asymmetrical.
4.example: Asymmetrical tires for better grip; a truck with an off-center cab for better visibility.

5.title: Merging
5.desc: Bring identical or similar objects closer together or assemble them into a single unit.
5.example: A double-bladed razor; multi-pane windows; parallel processing in computers.

6.title: Universality
6.desc: Make an object perform multiple functions, eliminating the need for other parts.
6.example: A Swiss Army knife; a sofa bed; a smartphone replacing camera, phone, and player.

7.title: Nested Doll (Matryoshka)
7.desc: Place one object inside another, which in turn is placed inside a third.
7.example: Telescopic antenna; stacked cups; retractable measuring tape.

8.title: Anti-Weight
8.desc: Compensate for the weight of an object by merging it with another object that provides lift.
8.example: Helium balloon for a heavy sign; hydrofoils on a boat.

9.title: Preliminary Anti-Action
9.desc: If it is necessary to perform an action with both harmful and useful effects, this action should be replaced by anti-actions to control the harmful effects.
9.example: Reinforced concrete (pre-stressed); buffer strips in fragile packaging.

10.title: Preliminary Action
10.desc: Perform the required action in advance (either fully or partially).
10.example: Self-adhesive wallpaper; pre-drilled holes for screws.

11.title: Beforehand Cushioning
11.desc: Prepare emergency means in advance to compensate for the relatively low reliability of an object.
11.example: Spare tire; airbag in a car; emergency exit lighting.

12.title: Equipotentiality
12.desc: In a potential field, limit condition changes (e.g., change operating conditions so an object doesn't need to be raised or lowered).
12.example: A canal lock; an oil changer that uses a vacuum instead of gravity drain.

13.title: The Other Way Round
13.desc: Invert the action; make movable parts fixed and fixed parts movable; turn the object upside down.
13.example: A treadmill (moving the surface instead of the person); rotating the tool instead of the part.

14.title: Spheroidality (Curvature)
14.desc: Go from rectilinear to curvilinear parts; use rollers, balls, spirals.
14.example: Computer mouse (trackball); arches in architecture for strength.

15.title: Dynamicity
15.desc: Allow the characteristics of an object to change to be optimal at each stage of operation.
15.example: Adjustable car seats; a folding umbrella; flexible keyboard.

16.title: Partial or Excessive Actions
16.desc: If 100% of an object is hard to achieve using a given solution, do slightly less or slightly more.
16.example: Paint a part by dipping it (excess paint is removed later); overfilling a container to ensure no air bubbles.

17.title: Another Dimension
17.desc: Move into a new dimension (1D to 2D, 2D to 3D); use multi-story arrangements.
17.example: Multi-layer circuit boards; stacked parking lots; spiral staircases.

18.title: Mechanical Vibration
18.desc: Cause an object to oscillate or vibrate; use ultrasonic frequencies.
18.example: Electric toothbrush; vibrating alert on a phone; ultrasonic cleaner.

19.title: Periodic Action
19.desc: Replace continuous action with periodic or impulse action.
19.example: Pulsed signal for turn indicators; an impact wrench; sirens.

20.title: Continuity of Useful Action
20.desc: Carry on work continuously; make all parts of an object work at full load, all the time.
20.example: Flywheel to store energy; a continuous casting machine in a factory.

21.title: Skipping
21.desc: Conduct a process or certain stages (e.g., destructive, harmful or hazardous operations) at high speed.
21.example: High-speed drilling to prevent tissue damage; flash photography.

22.title: Blessing in Disguise
22.desc: Use harmful factors (especially environmental) to obtain a positive effect.
22.example: Recycling waste heat for heating buildings; using centrifugal force to separate mixtures.

23.title: Feedback
23.desc: Introduce feedback to improve a process or action.
23.example: A thermostat for climate control; cruise control in a car; heart rate monitors.

24.title: Intermediary
24.desc: Use an intermediate carrier article or intermediary process.
24.example: A waiter's tray; a guitar pick; a power adapter.

25.title: Self-service
25.desc: Make an object service itself by performing auxiliary or repair operations.
25.example: Self-cleaning oven; self-sharpening blades; automatic software updates.

26.title: Copying
26.desc: Instead of an unavailable, expensive or fragile object, use simpler and inexpensive copies.
26.example: A flight simulator for training; virtual reality tours of museums.

27.title: Cheap Short-lived Objects
27.desc: Replace an expensive object with multiple inexpensive ones, compromising on longevity.
27.example: Disposable plates; single-use medical syringes; paper tissues.

28.title: Mechanics Substitution
28.desc: Replace a mechanical system with an optical, acoustic, thermal or electromagnetic system.
28.example: Laser pointers; acoustic parking sensors; touchscreens instead of buttons.

29.title: Pneumatics and Hydraulics
29.desc: Use gas and liquid parts of an object instead of solid parts.
29.example: Inflatable furniture; hydraulic jacks; air cushions in sneakers.

30.title: Flexible Shells and Thin Films
30.desc: Use flexible shells and thin films instead of three-dimensional structures.
30.example: Waterproof coatings; plastic wrap for food; solar sails.

31.title: Porous Materials
31.desc: Make an object porous or use auxiliary porous inserts.
31.example: Sponges for cleaning; foam insulation; breathable fabrics (Gore-Tex).

32.title: Color Changes
32.desc: Change the color of an object or its external environment.
32.example: Mood rings; photochromic lenses (transitions); thermal imaging.

33.title: Homogeneity
33.desc: Make objects interacting with a given object of the same material (or material with identical properties).
33.example: A diamond-tipped drill for cutting diamonds; wood glue for furniture.

34.title: Discarding and Recovering
34.desc: Make portions of an object that have fulfilled their functions go away or modify these directly during operation.

35.title: Parameter Changes
35.desc: Change an object's physical state (e.g., gas, liquid, solid), density, or temperature.
35.example: Freezing food for transport; liquid soap; compressed air.

36.title: Phase Transitions
36.desc: Use phenomena occurring during phase transitions (e.g., volume changes, heat emission or absorption).
36.example: Heat pipes for cooling electronics; phase-change materials in insulation.

37.title: Thermal Expansion
37.desc: Use thermal expansion (or contraction) of materials.
37.example: Bimetallic strips in thermostats; shrinking parts via cooling for tight fits.

38.title: Strong Oxidants
38.desc: Replace common air with enriched air; replace enriched air with oxygen.
38.example: Scuba diving with Nitrox; using ozone for water purification.

39.title: Inert Atmosphere
39.desc: Replace a neutral environment with an inert one; use a vacuum.
39.example: Argon welding; vacuum-sealed food packaging; nitrogen in tires.

40.title: Composite Materials
40.desc: Change from uniform to composite (multiple-layer) materials.
40.example: Fiberglass; carbon fiber bicycle frames; reinforced concrete.
